# 

# 

# Puelche: Agent-Based AI for Public Procurement Evaluation 

**Chapter 1: Description of the Problem or Opportunity and Solution.**

**1.1 Problem or Opportunity**

Public procurement, broadly defined as the acquisition of works, supplies, or services by government entities, represents a fundamental mechanism not only for market transactions but for the creation and protection of public value (Grandia et al., 2023). Beyond the act of purchasing, it serves as a strategic policy instrument through which governments deliver public services, allocate resources, and pursue broader social and economic goals (OECD, 2025c). Its scale underscores its importance: in 2023, public procurement accounted for 12.7% of Gross Domestic Product (GDP) and nearly 30% of total government expenditure across OECD countries, while in Latin America and the Caribbean it represented 17.4% of government spending in 2021 (OECD, 2024; OECD, 2025c).

Despite this strategic and fiscal importance, public procurement systems are frequently constrained by administrative inefficiencies, high transaction costs, and persistent procedural delays. The rigorous application of procurement legislation introduces substantial compliance burdens, and competitive tendering remains highly labor-intensive (Balaeva et al., 2022). Foundational assessments in the European Union estimated that administrative processes can consume approximately 1.4% of total contract value (PwC, 2011), while more complex mechanisms, such as two-stage tenders, may require more than 140 person-hours to execute (Balaeva et al., 2022). These burdens translate directly into delay: empirical evidence from the United Kingdom shows that roughly half of procurement processes experience overruns, with average durations exceeding 70 days (He et al., 2023). Furthermore, when institutional capacities are overwhelmed, these frictions can culminate in tender cancellations—a risk mitigated more effectively by robust administrative capacity for supplier outreach than by mere financial resources (Casady et al., 2023).

To address these inefficiencies, governments have historically pursued both regulatory reform and technological modernization. On the one hand, policymakers continue to revise procurement rules to simplify procedures and reduce administrative burden. For instance, the OECD Recommendation of the Council on Public Procurement advices governments to identify functional overlaps and operational silos (OECD, 2015), and the European Comission (2025) is  currently restructuring its public procurement directives to accelerate processes and significantly alleviate the administrative burden on both public entities and private contractors. On the other, public procurement systems have undergone successive waves of technological adoption, beginning with digitization through e-procurement platforms and moving toward broader digital transformation (Aboelazm & Dganni, 2025). By 2014, all OECD countries had implemented mandatory e-procurement platforms to centralize opportunities and tender documentation (OECD, 2025a). Building on this foundation public entities are now integrating sophisticated systems to lower costs and eliminate repetitive operations. Recent assessments indicate that 60% of OECD countries are actively deploying emerging technologies (primarily data analytics, artificial intelligence (AI), machine learning (ML), and robotic process automation) to significantly enhance administrative efficiency (OECD, 2025c; OECD, 2025d).

Yet an important gap remains. Within the procurement lifecycle, the substantive evaluation of bids continues to depend heavily on manual human labor, even as adjacent functions such as spending classification, anomaly detection, and routine supplier communication become increasingly automated (OECD, 2025a; Aboelazm & Dganni, 2025). While existing tools support peripheral administrative tasks, the application of Large Language Models (LLMs) and autonomous agentic systems to qualitative tender evaluation remains limited. Integrating these advanced, context-aware models into core procurement operations involves profound complexities, demanding rigorous adherence to traceability, public sector regulations, and strict ethical frameworks (OECD, 2025b). As a result, the development of robust and institutionally compatible AI-assisted evaluation systems remains an unresolved frontier in procurement modernization.

The Chilean case makes this challenge especially salient. Public procurement represented 14.3% of total government expenditure in 2021 (OECD, 2024), and in 2024 the national e-procurement platform, Mercado Público, processed more than two million purchase orders, mobilizing US$17.6 billion (Dirección ChileCompra, 2024). The Chilean framework, governed primarily by Law No. 19.886 and Decree No. 661, relies heavily on public tenders, which account for the largest share of procurement expenditure. Within this framework, the evaluation phase is both the most analytically demanding and the most institutionally sensitive stage, since proposals must be screened, scored against pre-established criteria, and translated into a legally binding award decision (Decreto 661, 2024, Chapter IV; Ley 19886, 2003). It is therefore not surprising that evaluation and award procedures account for the majority of procurement-related litigation (Tribunal de Contratación Pública, n.d.)..

This has led to measurable operational friction. In Figure 2, our analysis shows that in 2025, the standard tender required an average of 43.2 days from publication to award, while 34.2% of processes exceeded their estimated timelines, with average delays of 18.4 days and some cases extending as much as 345 days beyond the deadline. These delays are especially severe in high-stakes sectors such as public works and healthcare. At the same time, Chile offers unusually strong conditions for technological intervention. Mercado Público is recognized as one of the most developed e-procurement systems in Latin America, where early digital integration between 2006 and 2007 generated estimated administrative savings of 0.28% to 0.38% of government expenditure (OECD, 2024; Singer et al., 2009). Furthermore, ChileCompra continues to invest in data-driven monitoring, transparency infrastructure, and innovation, and has recently been awarded a prize for this by the Inter-American Network on Government Procurement (Dirección ChileCompra, 2025). In other words, Chile already possesses a mature digital and regulatory foundation, yet the qualitative evaluation of tenders remains a major manual bottleneck. That unresolved tension creates a strong case for the development of advanced AI-assisted evaluation support systems.

 **1.2 Proposed Solution**

Puelche is an AI–assisted system designed to support the evaluation of bids submitted to Chilean public tendering processes. The solution addresses the transaction costs, operational complexity, administrative burden, and time-intensive manual analysis currently required during bid evaluation, while preserving the transparency, traceability, and regulatory compliance mandated by Chilean public procurement law and established institutional oversight practices.

The system is conceived as a decision-support platform that augments—rather than replaces—institutional evaluation processes. It assists public officials in filtering, analyzing, and structuring information contained in tender documents and bidder proposals, allowing evaluation committees to concentrate on substantive technical judgment instead of repetitive document review. All administrative and evaluative decisions remain fully under human authority.

The primary users of the solution are acquisition units, evaluation committees (Comisiones Evaluadoras), and supporting legal and financial teams involved in procurement workflows. Puelche is delivered as a secure digital platform designed to integrate into existing institutional practices without altering formal procedures or governance structures, ensuring compatibility with current public-sector operations, documentation practices, and audit requirements.

The solution is designed as a modular, multi-agent system that decomposes the complex task of analyzing tender documents and bidder proposals into coordinated functional modules. Each module is responsible for a well-defined stage of information processing and evaluation support, enabling specialization of tasks while maintaining transparency and traceability across the analytical workflow. This modular architecture facilitates controlled system behavior and progressive integration into existing SERVIU evaluation practices.

At a high level, the system ingests tender documentation and submitted proposals, transforms heterogeneous and unstructured information into structured representations, supports the assessment of compliance with eligibility and evaluation criteria, and presents organized results through a user-oriented interface designed for human review and decision-making.

The system operates across five main functional modules aligned with the procurement evaluation workflow.

**I. Data Extraction Module**

The Data Extraction Module constitutes the informational foundation of the system by transforming heterogeneous procurement documentation into structured textual representations that can be analyzed by downstream agents. Its primary objective is to convert diverse document formats that can exist in a public procurement process—such as HTML pages, PDFs, Word files, and image-based documents—into raw textual content enriched with structural information describing the organization of the original document. This representation enables automated analysis while preserving traceability to the source materials, ensuring that any analytical result produced by the system can be linked back to the original document.

To address this challenge, the module integrates existing document-processing pipelines that combines several document intelligence techniques. Structured content obtained directly from the procurement platform (e.g., HTML pages from *Mercado Público*) as well as well-formed digital documents, such as native PDFs or MS Word files containing embedded text and layout structure, can be processed using document parsers extracting textual content and structural elements. In contrast, scanned or image-based documents require optical character recognition (OCR), layout analysis, and structural parsing to reconstruct textual and structural information. The module will integrate multiple document-processing tools and OCR engines, in order to adapt to different document formats and quality conditions encountered during deployment.

The output of this module consists of structured textual representations suitable for search, retrieval, and downstream reasoning. The extracted text is stored together with information describing document structure—such as sections, tables, images, and layout relationships—so that subsequent analytical modules can interpret the content in context. At the same time, the system preserves references linking extracted information to its original source (e.g., document identifiers, page numbers, and positions within the tender package), enabling transparent and traceable evaluation processes.

**II. Proposal Filtering Module**

The Proposal Filtering Module operationalizes the admissibility verification stage of the procurement workflow by transforming the structured outputs generated by the Data Extraction Module into a systematic compliance analysis aligned with the mandatory participation requirements defined in the tender rules.

Its primary objective is to assist evaluation committees in determining whether submitted bids satisfy the formal eligibility conditions specified in the procurement documentation (*bases administrativas* and *bases técnicas*), while ensuring full traceability to the original regulatory clauses to facilitate human oversight and enable the acquisition unit to make the final administrative decision.

The module ingests structured inputs from both tender documentation and bidder submissions. From the tender documents, it extracts mandatory participation requirements and formalizes admissibility rules through explicit rule-based logic, e.g., required documents and certificates. Each rule is linked to its originating clause—preserving document source and text span—ensuring traceability and support human oversight in the decision-making process. Bidder submissions are then evaluated against these rules to determine compliance.

The core verification tasks include confirming the existence of all required documents and declarations, validating that submitted materials are complete and properly structured, and enforcing additional formal compliance checks as defined in the tender requirements. The verification process combines deterministic validation mechanisms with controlled language-model–assisted reasoning where semantic interpretation is required. These analytical steps are executed through agents that invoke specialized capabilities from the **Compliance Skills and Tools Library**, such as document comparison tools, rule verification routines, and structured data extraction utilities.

The primary output of the Proposal Filtering Module is a structured admissibility matrix for each bidder, mapping: each mandatory requirement defined in the tender rules, the corresponding compliance assessment, and, the evidentiary references used for verification. These outputs are presented to acquisition teams through the User Interaction Module as a screening dashboard and downloadable analytical reports.

**III.Evaluation Support Module**

The Evaluation Support Module operates during the proposal evaluation phase conducted by the evaluation committee after submissions have been declared admissible. Its purpose is to assist evaluators in organizing, cross-referencing, and structuring relevant evidence in accordance with the scoring criteria defined in the tender rules (*Bases Económicas, Administrativas y Técnicas*), without autonomously issuing binding decisions.

**Identification of Evaluation Criteria and Scoring Methodology**

The module begins by identifying and extracting the evaluation criteria defined in the tender documentation, particularly those expressed through structured evaluation tables that specify how different aspects of a bid must be assessed. An example can be seen in Figure 4\. These tables describe the evaluation dimensions, associated scoring rules, and the evidentiary requirements expected from bidders. The system employs a set of specialized agents that focus on different classes of evaluation criteria, e.g., related to technical methodology, team qualifications, etc. Each agent detects and extracts the relevant criteria and represents them in a standardized internal schema.

Once the evaluation criteria have been identified, a planning agent produces an evaluation plan describing how the system should locate, verify, and organize the evidence required to assess the criterion. Because bidder submissions vary considerably, this step is necessary to retrieve all the relevant information. During this process, the agent may invoke capabilities provided by the Compliance Skills and Tools Library, such as document search procedures, entity identification tools, etc.

Then, the system decomposes the task into a set of specialized operations executed by subagents. Each subagent is designed to handle a specific component of the evaluation process, such as verifying professional experience, extracting qualifications from academic diplomas, and others. These subagents operate independently over the documents and generate evidence for scoring the requirements defined in the evaluation criteria.

Finally, the evidence produced by the subagents is consolidated and reviewed by a final evaluation agent, and produces a result for the corresponding evaluation criterion. This process results in the completion of the evaluation table associated linking the resulting score and an assessment to the documentary evidence used to support it. Figure 5 shows the general framework the agents of this module (and the filtering module) uses.

**IV. Compliance Skills and Tools Library**

The **Compliance Skills and Tools Library** functions as a shared capability layer that supports the activities performed by the agents operating within the **Proposal Filtering Module** and the **Evaluation Support Module**. Rather than embedding complex procedures directly within each agent, this module centralizes specialized operations. Agents responsible for interpreting procurement rules, verifying admissibility conditions, or structuring evaluation evidence access this module when their tasks require specialized processing capabilities.

The library provides these capabilities in a **modular and reusable form**, enabling agents to invoke standardized tools when performing complex or specialized operations such as document analysis, rule verification, or score computation. Within this framework, the architecture distinguishes between **skills** and **tools**. *Tools* correspond to concrete computational functions that execute well-defined operations, such as parsing tabular data, or computing evaluation scores. *Skills*, in contrast, represent structured procedural capabilities that encapsulate domain expertise, instructions, and reusable workflows that agents can load and apply when performing complex tasks relevant to procurement evaluation, such as verifying compliance with eligibility rules or extracting and organizing evidence related to evaluation criteria.

To ensure **interoperability and long-term maintainability**, the Compliance Skills and Tools Library is implemented following emerging standards for agent capability exposure and tool invocation, particularly the **Agent Skills Protocol** and the **Model Context Protocol (MCP)**. These frameworks provide standardized interfaces through which language models and agentic systems can discover and invoke external capabilities in a structured and auditable manner. Adopting these protocols ensures that the system remains compatible with evolving LLMs and agent orchestration frameworks.

Beyond its role in the current architecture, the library is conceived as a **long-term capability layer for agentic solutions operating in public-sector administrative environments**. By accumulating reusable analytical skills, this design significantly reduces engineering effort and cost when extending the system to new use cases or institutions, enabling faster prototyping and deployment of additional agent-based applications across public-sector processes.

**V. User Interface and Traceability Layer**

The User Interface and Traceability Layer provides the operational interface through which evaluation committees, acquisition units, and administrative personnel interact with the Puelche system. Through this layer, users access and review the outputs generated by the underlying modules, allowing the system to integrate into existing institutional workflows while maintaining human authority over procurement evaluation decisions.

A central feature of the interface is the explicit presentation of traceability. All analytical outputs displayed by the system are grounded in the documentary evidence from which they were derived. The interface allows users to navigate directly from structured assessments to the specific sections of tender documents or bidder submissions that support those conclusions facilitating verification by evaluation committees.

Finally, the platform integrates with the government system’s role-based access control framework. This integration regulates permissions for reviewing, annotating, validating, or exporting evaluation results, ensuring that system use remains consistent with institutional governance structures and accountability requirements within public procurement processes.

**VI. Optimization of Agentic Modules**

The **Proposal Filtering Module** and the **Evaluation Support Module** operate within an agentic architecture whose capabilities are progressively improved through an **optimization process**. Rather than relying exclusively on static prompt engineering, the system adopts a structured improvement cycle in which the behavior of the agentic harness is iteratively refined using evidence derived from previously human evaluated procurement cases. This process enables the system to evolve as additional procurement data becomes available while preserving transparency and traceability in system behavior.

In the Puelche architecture, each procurement case is first processed through a **case-level execution loop**, producing structured operational outputs. The **optimization loop** operates across a collection of procurement cases for which human evaluation results are available. After a case has been processed by the system, the generated outputs are compared against the corresponding human committee judgments. Discrepancies between the system’s output and reference evaluation are then analyzed to determine their underlying cause.

In this process, the **agentic harness** is optimized, which is composed of three principal components:

* The **Compliance Skills and Tools Library**, contains reusable procedures used by the agents of the system.  
* The **supervisory policy** governs how the system selects which skill to apply to each evaluation criterion, manages retries, and determines when a criterion should be escalated for human review.  
* The **representation substrate** defines how procurement documents, tables, and sections are reconstructed and presented to the agents for analysis.

Within the context of the current proposal, this optimization framework will be applied primarily to the **Proposal Filtering Module**, the **Evaluation Support Module** and the skills in the **Compliance Skills and Tools Library**, which represent the most knowledge-intensive components of the system. And over time, this process will expand the library of reusable capabilities available to the agents, progressively increasing the coverage and reliability of AI-assisted procurement evaluation.

**Current Development Status and Target Maturity of the Solution**

At the time of project submission, the **Puelche system has reached Technology Readiness Level (TRL) 4**, corresponding to technology validated in a laboratory environment for a bounded but representative set of evaluation tasks within the public procurement workflow.[^1] At this stage, the project has successfully demonstrated the technical feasibility of applying an AI-assisted agent–based architecture to support structured analysis of procurement documentation and bidder proposals, including reproducible results on real procurement files and controlled comparison against prior human-reviewed outcomes.

The current prototype focuses on the **implementation of the Evaluation Support Module**, and has already been exercised on several representative documentary evaluation tasks. These include evaluation criteria such as: the assessment of the proposed executive team curriculum (equipo profesional propuesto), methodology and work-program review, execution-period assessment, integrity-program verification, environmental-impact verification, bidder-experience measurement, and accreditation of software-based technical capabilities. These tasks have been tested in several real procurement contexts, including training and coaching services, technical inspection and advisory consultancies, social consultancy and accompaniment services, and operational service contracts. They were selected as the initial laboratory scope because they represent some of the most complex and time-consuming components of the procurement evaluation process. Evaluators must verify professional qualifications and documented experience using curriculum vitae and supporting evidence, including diplomas, certifications, employment contracts, service invoices, and other annexed documentation submitted by bidders, review bounded sections of technical proposals against explicit rubrics, and verify recurring documentary criteria through annexes and supporting evidence. The prototype therefore demonstrates the **core technical feasibility of the proposed system architecture**, including document processing, structured evidence extraction, and evaluation support capabilities. Importantly, the Evaluation Support Module constitutes the **most technically demanding component of the overall system**, since the analytical processes required for evaluation involve the highest level of complexity within the procurement workflow. A visual example of the processing of a tender document by the prototype can be seen at this [link](https://mbosley.github.io/standalone/serviu_end_to_end_single_column_mockup_2026-03-18.html).

The proposed project will expand this prototype into a **fully integrated decision-support platform capable of supporting the broader procurement evaluation process**. To achieve this objective, the project will focus on several key technological developments. First, the remaining system modules described in the architecture will be implemented, enabling the system to support earlier stages of the evaluation workflow. Second, the **Evaluation Support Module will be extended to additional evaluation dimensions**, including the analysis of project methodology, environmental policies, gender parity indicators, between others. These extensions will significantly broaden the scope of automated analytical assistance provided by the platform. Finally, the project will address **system integration and operational validation**, ensuring that the complete platform can operate coherently within real procurement workflows and institutional environments.

By the end of the project, the **Puelche system is expected to reach Technology Readiness Level (TRL) 7**, corresponding to a **system prototype demonstrated in an operational environment**. Achieving this level of maturity will validate the system’s capacity to operate under real institutional conditions. Reaching TRL-7 will position the solution for **precommercial deployment and institutional adoption**, enabling its implementation in organizations such as **SERVIU and other public procurement units** that face similar administrative and analytical challenges in bid evaluation.

**Conditions of Use**

Puelche is designed to operate as an institutional decision-support platform embedded within the existing procurement workflow of SERVIU Metropolitano. The system is intended exclusively for use by authorized public officials involved in procurement processes, including acquisition units, evaluation committees (Comisiones Evaluadoras), and supporting legal or financial teams. Access is restricted to authenticated institutional users and governed through role-based permissions.

The system supports internal activities during the stages of admissibility review and technical evaluation. It does not interact directly with bidders, does not publish information externally, and does not alter the formal procedures established in the procurement documentation. All legally binding determinations—including eligibility validation, scoring decisions, proposal ranking, adjudication, and contract approval—remain under the authority of the designated public officials.

**Operational Requirements.** For proper operation, the system requires digital access to tender documentation and bidder submissions in standard electronic formats, secure infrastructure compliant with public-sector cybersecurity policies, authenticated user access managed by SERVIU, and secure web access for authorized evaluators. The system is designed to integrate with existing document repositories and procurement workflows without requiring changes to formal governance procedures.

**Human-in-the-Loop Supervision**. Puelche operates under a strict human-in-the-loop supervision model. All system outputs constitute analytical recommendations or structured summaries rather than binding decisions. Users may review, validate, annotate, modify, or reject system-generated outputs. Final determinations regarding admissibility, scoring, and proposal ranking remain exclusively under the authority of the evaluation committee.

**Transparency and Data Governance.** The platform maintains detailed logs of system operations and user interactions, including timestamps, validation actions, and versioned evaluation records. These logs support audit processes while preserving full transparency of analytical steps. All data processing will comply with institutional data governance policies. When external AI services are required for inference tasks, processing is configured under strict data protection safeguards, including zero-data-retention configurations and contractual restrictions preventing reuse of institutional data. No bidder information will be used for LLM training.

**Expected Performance Indicators**

The evaluation framework for Puelche is structured around three complementary dimensions: efficacy, efficiency, and institutional acceptance. 

From an efficacy perspective, the system will be assessed based on its ability to accurately and consistently support admissibility and technical evaluation tasks. **Inadmissibility accuracy** measures the proportion of system classifications that align with expert evaluations or validated ground truth, reflecting the system’s reliability in identifying admissible and inadmissible bids. In parallel, the **evaluation consistency rate** captures the level of agreement between Puelche-generated assessments and independent expert evaluations in the technical review process, using statistical measures such as Cohen’s Kappa, Pearson correlation, and F1 score. Complementing these, **evaluation criteria coverage** measures the extent to which the system operationalizes the full set of defined technical evaluation criteria, quantified as the number of criteria types supported by Puelche that also meet the required inter-rater reliability threshold. This indicator reflects the system’s flexibility and constitutes a key comparative advantage over more rigid or narrowly scoped alternatives.

Efficiency indicators focus on quantifying operational improvements in procurement workflows. **Evaluation time reduction** measures the decrease in time required for both proposal filtering and technical evaluation when using Puelche, compared to standard manual procedures. **Cost per evaluation reduction** assesses the corresponding decrease in resource utilization, expressed both in monetary terms and as a percentage change relative to historical averages. These indicators capture the system’s contribution to reducing administrative burden and accelerating evaluation cycles.

Finally, qualitative indicators assess the system’s integration into institutional practice. **User experience and institutional acceptance** will be evaluated through structured feedback from evaluation committee members and acquisition personnel, focusing on usability, transparency, trust, and alignment with existing workflows. **Leadership and organizational satisfaction** will capture perceptions of decision quality, procedural consistency, and accountability from supervisory stakeholders. As no directly comparable technological solutions currently exist, performance will be evaluated relative to baseline human processes, emphasizing demonstrable improvements and institutional validation rather than benchmarking against alternative systems.

**Key Differentiating Attributes of the Puelche System**

Puelche introduces a novel technological approach to supporting public procurement evaluation by integrating agent-based AI with traceable rule-based analysis designed for the institutional and regulatory context of Chilean public procurement. To the best of our knowledge, no existing system applies an agentic architecture to assist evaluation committees during the bid evaluation stage of public tender processes. Current tools rely mainly on RAG or Supervised Fine Tuning techniques, which lack the flexibility an agentic system can provide. In contrast, Puelche directly addresses this issue by using state-of-the-art structured document information retrieval and augmented capabilities through our specialized agents using tools, skills and sub-agents.

A central differentiating feature of the system is the use of explicit, formalized procurement rules as a mechanism to ensure interpretability and transparency in the evaluation process. Rather than relying exclusively on opaque model-based judgments, Puelche extracts and structures admissibility and evaluation rules from tender documentation and represents them as explicit compliance conditions that guide the system’s analytical procedures. This rule-based layer provides a clear and auditable structure through which evaluation logic can be understood, inspected, and verified by human evaluators and oversight bodies.

Finally, the Puelche architecture incorporates an outer-loop optimization framework that allows the system to progressively improve its performance over time. This capability represents a key differentiating element of the proposed solution, as most existing agent-based systems rely on static prompt configurations that remain fixed once deployed. In contrast, Puelche is designed to evolve as additional procurement cases and human evaluation outcomes become available, enabling systematic refinement of the agentic system’s behavior, improving the system and making it more reliable through time.

**Use of Patented and Licensed Technologies**

The proposed solution does not rely on components protected by patents. Its architecture is primarily based on open standards, widely available technologies, and modular integrations that do not impose proprietary restrictions on core system functionality. However, certain optional components may involve the use of third-party technologies that are commercially licensed. These include, for example, proprietary document parsing and OCR tools, as well as LLMs accessed through usage-based pricing schemes (e.g., token-based APIs). The use of such technologies does not imply dependency, as functionally equivalent open-source alternatives are available and can be integrated if required, ensuring flexibility and avoiding vendor lock-in.

**1.3 State-of-the-Art Analysis**

**a. Alternative Solutions Under Development or Existing**

*Declaration of Funding:* At time of submission this project has not been submitted to another government funding program in Chile.

**1\. Technology Gaps in Public Procurement: The Global Application Landscape**

Administrative efficiency, transaction costs, and procedural delays in public procurement have historically been addressed by transitioning to e-procurement platforms (OECD, 2025a). However, traditional digitalization has reached an operational ceiling when handling highly complex purchasing processes. Consequently, the integration of emerging technologies has become a vital frontier. According to a 2024 OECD survey, 58% of member countries use emerging technologies to drive administrative efficiency, with 41% employing AI and ML to support procurement activities, and 15% actively developing new AI applications (OECD, 2025a; OECD, 2025d). Despite this institutional momentum, academic literature highlights that AI adoption in procurement remains in its infancy, lacking practical deployment in operational supply tasks (Guida et al., 2023; Andersson et al., 2025). This generates an "automation mismatch" (Bin Amjad, 2025): while private suppliers increasingly leverage Generative AI (GenAI) to mass-produce complex bids in record time, government evaluators remain tethered to manual, time-consuming review systems, maintaining legacy bureaucratic workloads and administrative burdens.

**2\. The Global Landscape: Application Asymmetry**

An analysis of existing governmental AI applications worldwide reveals a strong trend toward predictive analytics and conversational assistance, leaving core evaluative tasks unaddressed.

**Fraud Detection and Risk Management:** Predictive ML systems are used to flag corruption risks and spending anomalies. Examples include: Ukraine’s *DoZorro* software (Transparency International Ukraine, 2018); Singapore’s, Technology and Research's risk-detection models (GovInsider, 2016); Brazil’s *Labcontas* project for auditing (Gomes, 2018); ChileCompra’s *Observatorio de Compras Públicas* (Dirección ChileCompra, n.d.; Granados, 2024); and, Utah’s employs AI to reduce purchasing bias and budget overruns (Smith, 2018).

**Document Processing and Categorization:** AI is leveraged to categorize public spending, as seen in Finland's Hansel Oy and Australia's New South Wales Data Analytics Centre (Microsoft, 2018; European Commission, 2020). Other systems assist in contract preparation by recommending standard clauses based on historical data (e.g., US HHS Accelerate \[European Commission, 2020\]) or scraping and digitizing institutional documents (Romania’s National Public Procurement Agency \[World Bank, 2023\]).

**Conversational Assistance:** Chatbots are deployed to assist suppliers and employees, such as "Ask Laura" in El Paso (Zima, 2017); PAIGE in San Francisco (Wood, 2018); the US Air Force/IBM procurement advisor (RT, 2016); and the North Carolina DIT assistant (NCDIT, 2024).

**Private Sector Solutions:** This market exhibits higher maturity level with tools like *AutogenAI* for rapid bid drafting (AutogenAI, n.d.), and *Sievo* for supplier analytics (Sievo, n.d.).

This aligns with recent evidence: almost 80% of GenAI applications in supply chains are concentrated in planning, information management, and supplier discovery, while phases, such as the evaluation of proposals, remain severely underrepresented (Bahroun et al., 2026; Guida et al., 2023).

**3\. Public Sector Barriers and the Need for "Governance by Design"**

If the technological capacity to optimize evaluation exists, why is it not implemented? The literature points to institutional and ethical barriers. The slow adoption of AI is largely explained by an institutional inertia rooted in risk aversion, fear of litigation, and the personal liability of procurement officials operating under strict transparency mandates (Bin Amjad, 2025). Furthermore, public agencies face stringent IT security and compliance regulations that restrict the use of cloud-based, commercial AI solutions (Andersson et al., 2025).

Ethically, the public procurement of third-party AI poses severe risks. Utilizing opaque, "black-box" commercial AI inadvertently transfers regulatory power and policy formulation to private vendors, threatening citizens' rights, accountability, and fairness (Hickok, 2024). Additionally, AI can conflict with public strategic goals, such as green public procurement objectives (Andhov et al., 2025). Consequently, governments cannot rely on generic commercial AI; they require highly specialized, explainable architectures built with "governance by design"—ensuring strict human-in-the-loop oversight and fully traceable decision-making to mitigate bias and ensure regulatory compliance (Bahroun et al., 2026; Hickok, 2024).

**4\. The Opportunity for Innovation: LLMs Proofs-of-Concept and Implementations**

The qualitative and technical evaluation of bids remains a profound operational bottleneck. Reviewing extensive regulatory compliance proposals requires significant manual effort from Subject Matter Experts (SMEs), resulting in severe delays and leaving processes vulnerable to human error (Aboelazm & Dganni, 2025). To address this, recent applied research has focused on testing customized Artificial Intelligence architectures. While live production deployments for substantive evaluation remain scarce, several studies have successfully validated proofs of concept and prototype systems using historical tender data to assess technical viability.

Existing solutions can be broadly categorized into three lines of work. First, several studies focus on structured information extraction from heterogeneous tender documents. These approaches address tasks such as optical character recognition (OCR), clause identification, and entity extraction. For instance, Roshan and Alam (2025) combine LLMs with OCR and human-in-the-loop validation to process unstructured tender documents, achieving an F1-score of 90% in data extraction and clause matching, while reducing evaluation time by 60% at low operational cost. Similarly, Li et al. (2025) integrate Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) with vector-based retrieval to perform entity recognition, reporting an F1-score of 93.10. While these results are significant, they target comparatively narrow subproblems and do not extend to higher-level evaluative reasoning. Their relevance lies primarily in establishing the reliability of upstream capabilities—such as document parsing and semantic extraction—which are necessary but not sufficient for end-to-end bid evaluation.

A second line of work attempts to directly support or automate evaluation tasks. Agarwal et al. (2026) propose a system combining OCR, LLMs, and task-specific machine learning models trained on over 1,000 tenders, achieving 90–95% accuracy and reducing manual workload by 45%. However, this approach relies on predefined and static predefined evaluation criteria, limiting its applicability in real-world procurement settings where evaluation frameworks are dynamically defined within each tender’s regulatory documents. This constraint significantly reduces generalizability and restricts deployment to highly standardized procurement scenarios. Consequently, while these results demonstrate that AI-assisted evaluation is achievable, they also highlight the limitations of rigid, template-dependent architectures.

A third line of work focuses on augmenting human evaluators through Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) frameworks. Sofat and Sodhi (2024) present a system that leverages open-source LLMs to answer factual compliance queries, enabling evaluators to achieve over 80% accuracy while reducing response times from hours to seconds. These approaches effectively enhance information access and retrieval but remain limited to query-based assistance. They do not provide structured, traceable, or comprehensive evaluation outputs aligned with formal scoring criteria, nor do they address the orchestration of multi-step evaluative reasoning.

Taken together, the literature provides strong evidence of the technical viability of individual components required for AI-assisted tender evaluation—namely document understanding, information extraction, and query-based reasoning. However, it also reveals a clear gap: the absence of integrated, end-to-end systems capable of performing structured, criteria-driven evaluation under real regulatory conditions. In particular, there is a lack of agent-based architectures that can dynamically interpret tender-specific rules, coordinate multi-step reasoning processes, and produce auditable, human-aligned evaluation outputs.

To the best of our knowledge, no existing solution fully addresses these requirements, nor are there implementations explicitly adapted to the regulatory, procedural, and document-specific characteristics of the Chilean public procurement system. This gap defines a concrete opportunity for innovation.

**5\. Puelche Differentiation**

Unlike existing national and international alternatives that focus on conversational assistance, conventional RAG pipelines, or predictive risk scoring, Puelche introduces a **multi-agent** decision-support system specifically designed to address the most labor-intensive bottleneck: qualitative bid evaluation that it also optimized for performance. This method has proven more efficient with different types of tasks and data (Maragheh et al., 2025; Neha & Bhati, 2025).  By embedding a *governance-by-design* architecture, the system is natively aligned with data privacy requirements and public procurement regulations, ensuring full traceability, auditability, and human-in-the-loop control. This enables a legally compliant and operationally robust transition from isolated proofs of concept to scalable, real-world governmental deployment.

From a scientific standpoint, the project is innovative by evaluating the system under real procurement conditions. It will generate evidence on the operational impact of AI-assisted evaluation—measuring accuracy, efficiency, and cost/time reductions—while also enabling in-depth analysis of system behavior, human–AI interaction, and decision consistency in high-stakes administrative contexts.

**6\. The CENIA Ecosystem**

*Puelche* directly addresses these rigid institutional barriers by creating a transparent, auditable, and locally sovereign agentic AI system specifically tailored to Chilean administrative law. Within the national ecosystem, the National Center for Artificial Intelligence (CENIA) is leading the transformation of public procurement to achieve the end-to-end modernization vision recommended by the OECD (2025a). CENIA is currently executing a Google.org-funded project ("Hazlo con IA") in collaboration with four municipalities to develop an AI-assisted MVP for drafting technical and administrative tender bases, guiding text proposals, and alerting completeness gaps to reduce initial rework. Simultaneously, CENIA is developing a pilot with SERVIU RM to automate the final contract generation phase by deploying an agentic tool that verifies required supplier documents and drafts contract templates for legal review. *Puelche* represents a step forward to cover the procurement cycle.

b. Intellectual and Industrial Property Analysis and Existing Market Products

To identify similar protected technologies, we conducted a global analysis across Google Patents, WIPO PATENTSCOPE, and Espacenet, combining keywords (e.g., public procurement, LLMs) with targeted patent classifications like G06Q (administrative/management systems) and G06N (artificial intelligence).

 Table 1 Intellectual property[^2]

| Patent Number | Title and Owner of the patent | Advantages of Puelche over the patent |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| CN119624385A | A method and system for automatically reviewing bidding documents based on artificial intelligence \- Guizhou Shanfeng Engineering Consulting Co ltd | Unlike this patent, Puelche does not require an ML training procedure to operate, enabling faster deployment with lower costs and reduced computational demands. This also increases system flexibility, as it eliminates the need for retraining to accommodate new use cases or institutions.  |
| US16828305 | Autonomous bidder solicitation and selection system \- Saudi Arabian Oil Co | This patent focuses on automating bidder selection with minimal human supervision, Puelche acts strictly as a decision-support system, ensuring the final resolutive authority remains human, necessary for Chilean regulation. |
| CN119205288A | An Internet-based intelligent bidding management platform \- Shenzhen Xiangke Internet Technology Co ltd | Unlike this patent, Puelche does not require an ML training procedure to train a scoring module, enabling faster deployment and lower costs. Increasing system flexibility, as it eliminates the need for retraining to accommodate new use cases or institutions. |
| CN116703337B | Project document examination system and method based on artificial intelligence technology- Jinxiandai Information Industry Co ltd | While this patent utilizes LLMs as generic document checkers to spot basic defects and data omissions, Puelche is a specialized multi-agent system designed exclusively to evaluate complex compliance against specific public tender rules. |
| CN119991032A | A bidding information feedback comparison system based on big data \- First Engineering Co Ltd of China Railway Electrification Engineering Group Co Ltd | This patent primarily integrates NLP and AI to analyze and judge the quantitative level of bid evaluation prices. Puelche's unique value proposition targets the far more complex bottleneck: the non-price, qualitative assessment of public tenders.  |

Some patents were excluded as they target narrower procurement use cases rather than comprehensive qualitative bid evaluation, which is the focus of this analysis. This is the case for: predicting optimal bid amount (KR102514993B1), supplier risk assessment (CN119850315B, US20170286870A1), generating initial bidding documents (CN110232177B), and identify collusion (CN113129118A).

3. Regulations.

An exhaustive analysis of national and international regulations was conducted to ensure the project's strict alignment with the legal and ethical standards of the Chilean public sector. This comprehensive review encompassed the core tenets of public procurement, alongside critical frameworks governing information security, cybersecurity, data privacy, and Artificial Intelligence. By incorporating global standards—such as the EU AI Act and OECD/UNESCO recommendations—and proactively aligning with the risk-based Chilean AI Law currently under legislative discussion (Proyecto de Ley Boletín N° 16821-19, 2024), this dense regulatory landscape serves as a definitive blueprint rather than a restrictive barrier. Adopting a "Compliance by Design" approach ensures that strict legal constraints regarding opaque algorithms, data sovereignty, and algorithmic bias do not limit our innovation; instead, they enable it by providing the exact specifications required for secure institutional adoption. This validates our strategy of building an advisory, human-in-the-loop platform that prioritizes traceability, robust audit logging, and the isolation of confidential data. For a detailed, norm-by-norm breakdown of this compliance strategy, please refer to the attached Regulation Analysis Appendix (at Attached Material \> Regulation Analysis Appendix.pdf ).

**1.4 Users and target audience transfer/massification strategy:**

**Project Type and Justification**

Based on the proposed solution and its expected technological outputs, this initiative is classified as a **Precompetitive Technological Research Project**. The project seeks to develop and validate an AI-assisted system to support admissibility review and bid evaluation in public procurement processes. Its technological core is an agent-based architecture capable of processing heterogeneous procurement documentation, extracting structured information from tender rules and bidder submissions, and supporting acquisition units, evaluation committees, and associated legal and financial teams through transparent and traceable analytical tools. At the time of application, the technology corresponds to TRL 4, reflecting a validated proof of concept for a bounded set of evaluation tasks. The project aims to advance the solution to TRL 7, demonstrating the system in a relevant operational environment and generating the technical validation required for subsequent transfer and adoption.

Because procurement procedures are highly standardized across public institutions, the proposed technology has strong potential for scalability within the public sector. Following validation, the system could be transferred through a GovTech Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) model, enabling progressive adoption by public institutions and An important intermediate market actor is the Dirección de Compras y Contratación Pública (ChileCompra), through its administration of the Mercado Público platform. Integration of the system as a complementary analytical layer within that ecosystem would create a pathway for large-scale deployment across the national procurement system. In the longer term, the technology may also support complementary applications for private suppliers that participate regularly in public tenders, particularly those requiring assistance with regulatory compliance and bid preparation, and may later be adaptable to other countries operating digital public procurement platforms.

**End Users, Intermediate Market, and Target Actors**

The proposed technology addresses several categories of actors involved in public procurement. Its primary end users are public officials directly engaged in procurement evaluation, including acquisition units, evaluation committees (*Comisiones Evaluadoras*), and supporting legal and financial teams. These actors would interact directly with the system during admissibility review and technical evaluation, benefiting from automated document analysis, structured evidence organization, and decision-support tools. The principal institutional adopters are public entities that regularly conduct procurement procedures and therefore require support for document-intensive evaluation tasks.

An important intermediate market actor is the centralized public procurement platform managed by the Dirección de Compras y Contratación Pública (ChileCompra). Integration of the system as a complementary analytical tool within the Mercado Público ecosystem would enable large-scale deployment across the national procurement system, making the technology accessible to multiple public institutions through a centralized digital infrastructure. In addition to public-sector users, the technology presents potential applications for private-sector actors participating in procurement markets, particularly companies that regularly submit bids to public tenders. These organizations face significant administrative complexity when preparing proposals and ensuring compliance with procurement regulations. Analytical tools derived from the proposed system could assist private suppliers in verifying regulatory compliance, organizing bid documentation, and improving the quality of tender submissions.

**Chilean Market Size and Accessibility**

The Chilean public procurement system constitutes a large and highly standardized institutional market for the proposed technology. In 2024, Mercado Público processed more than 2.03 million purchase orders, mobilizing USD 17.643 billion and involving 1,086 public buying institutions and more than 110,000 suppliers (Dirección ChileCompra, 2024). Public tenders are the most relevant mechanism for the evaluation processes addressed by the proposed system, accounting for approximately USD 12.584 billion in transactions in 2024, equivalent to 71% of total procurement volume (Ministerio de Vivienda y Urbanismo, n.d.).

On this basis, the market can be described through three levels. The Total Addressable Market (TAM) corresponds to the 1,086 public buying institutions operating through Mercado Público. Under the preliminary subscription model described for the platform, this implies approximately USD 17.2 million in recurring annual revenue potential, excluding implementation services. The Serviceable Available Market (SAM) corresponds to the initial sectoral deployment context, namely the network of 16 regional SERVIU institutions under MINVU, equivalent to approximately USD 253,440 in annual recurring revenue. The Serviceable Obtainable Market (SOM) corresponds to a conservative initial expansion scenario in which four public institutions adopt the platform during the first stage after pilot validation, representing approximately USD 63,360 in recurring annual revenue.

Market accessibility is reinforced by the high level of institutional standardization in Chilean procurement procedures and by the central role of Mercado Público as national procurement infrastructure. This accessibility has increased further as recent regulatory reforms have incorporated more than 250 additional public entities into the platform since 2024 (Dirección ChileCompra, 2025a). Taken together, these conditions create a favorable environment for sectoral validation, progressive national adoption, and later expansion.

**Technology Transfer, Adoption, and Massification Pathway**

The transfer strategy follows a staged institutional pathway. The first stage consists of pilot validation in a real operational context, where the system will run in shadow mode alongside existing procurement workflows. Under this scheme, the platform will process tender documentation and generate analytical outputs while evaluation committees continue to perform the official review under standard procedures. This arrangement enables direct comparison between system outputs and human evaluations, allowing the project to measure consistency, time reduction, and usability without displacing institutional authority. The second stage consists of sectoral adoption in institutions that share similar procurement procedures and regulatory conditions, beginning with SERVIU-type organizations. A third stage consists of possible larger-scale deployment through centralized procurement infrastructure, particularly via integration with Mercado Público or related public-sector digital channels. Once the technology has been validated and initial institutional pathways have been established, broader transfer may proceed through a GovTech SaaS model supported by technical maintenance, institutional outreach, and targeted adoption partnerships.

**Economic, Social, and Institutional Benefits**

The proposed technology is expected to generate benefits across operational, institutional, and public-value dimensions. From an operational perspective, it can reduce the time and effort required to review large volumes of bidder documentation, verify admissibility conditions, cross-reference supporting evidence, and structure evaluation outputs. Preliminary institutional evidence illustrates the scale of this burden: procurement procedures conducted by SERVIU RM in 2025 included 131 public tenders involving 2,081 participating bidders, each associated with an average of approximately 1,000 pages of documentation (Dirección ChileCompra, 2026). At current estimates of document processing and language-model inference, automated analysis of approximately 1,000 pages would involve an operational cost of roughly USD 4.5 per tender, implying annual computational costs well below the labor costs of manual review in comparable settings.

Beyond efficiency, the system also offers institutional benefits by strengthening transparency, consistency, and traceability in procurement evaluation. By organizing evidentiary references and evaluation criteria into structured analytical outputs, it supports internal verification and institutional accountability while allowing public officials to concentrate on higher-value activities such as deliberation, procurement planning, and quality control. These gains also carry broader public value: reducing delays in evaluation can accelerate the implementation of public programs and infrastructure projects, while clearer traceability can strengthen confidence in procurement decisions and support wider public-sector modernization efforts.

**Entry Barriers, Risks, and Externalities**

The adoption of AI-assisted decision-support tools in public procurement involves regulatory, organizational, and technological barriers. From a regulatory perspective, any such system must remain strictly subordinate to human authority, given the legal and administrative requirements governing procurement evaluation. The proposed architecture is designed accordingly: it operates under a human-in-the-loop model in which system outputs function as analytical recommendations and evidence summaries rather than binding decisions. From an organizational perspective, public institutions may initially be cautious in adopting automated analytical tools within sensitive administrative workflows. For that reason, the project incorporates a progressive validation strategy based on pilot deployment and shadow-mode testing, allowing institutional users to assess performance without altering formal procedures. From a technical perspective, the main barrier lies in the heterogeneity and uneven quality of procurement documentation, including scanned files, complex tables, and mixed document formats. The proposed system addresses this challenge through document intelligence pipelines that combine OCR, layout-aware parsing, and structured information extraction while preserving traceability to original source materials.

At the same time, the technology may generate positive externalities beyond its immediate use case. By reducing evaluation delays and improving documentary traceability, it can support more efficient procurement processes, stronger oversight, and more consistent application of evaluation criteria. In the longer term, it may also contribute to broader public-sector modernization by demonstrating a practical and institutionally responsible model for the use of advanced AI systems in administrative decision-support contexts.

**1.5 Associated Entities and Development Strategy**

**Partner Entity: SERVIU Región Metropolitana (SERVIU RM)**

SERVIU Región Metropolitana (SERVIU RM) is the regional operational arm of the Ministry of Housing and Urbanism (MINVU), responsible for implementing national housing and urban development policies in Chile’s most densely populated region. As part of its institutional mandate, SERVIU RM manages and executes a significant volume of public procurement processes related to social housing construction, urban infrastructure, land development, and technical consultancy services, primarily through Chile’s national e-procurement platform, Mercado Público. Within Chile’s public procurement system, regional operational agencies such as SERVIU RM play a critical role in executing high-value public tenders. In this context, SERVIU RM conducts a substantial number of public bidding processes each year, many of which involve complex technical specifications, multidisciplinary evaluation committees, and strict regulatory compliance requirements under Law No. 19,886 and its recent amendments.

SERVIU RM will act as the project’s early adopter and validation partner, providing structured access to historical tender documents, bidding submissions, evaluation reports, and adjudication resolutions necessary for system training, calibration, and benchmarking. In addition, SERVIU RM will designate a technical counterpart team to participate in co-design workshops, validation sessions, and iterative testing phases. The system will be deployed under a controlled “shadow-mode validation” framework, operating in parallel to human evaluation committees in selected public tenders in order to measure concordance rates, time reduction, administrative workload impact, and admissibility detection accuracy. This collaboration ensures that the solution is developed and validated in a real operational public procurement environment, strengthening its technological maturity and market readiness. 

**Strategic Development Plan.** 

The strategic development plan of the project follows a staged approach that connects technological development, institutional validation, knowledge transfer, and progressive adoption within the public procurement ecosystem. The strategy is designed to ensure that the proposed technology evolves from a research prototype into an operational decision-support system validated in real institutional environments and capable of being progressively deployed across public-sector organizations.

The **first stage** of the strategy focuses on technological and institutional co-design. During this phase, the system architecture will be designed and implemented in close collaboration with SERVIU RM, which will act as the associated institutional partner and early adopter. Through interviews, workshops, and iterative validation sessions with procurement officials, the project will characterize the operational workflow of public procurement evaluation processes and translate this knowledge into a formalized computational architecture based on document intelligence, rule-based verification mechanisms, and multi-agent analytical workflows.

The **second stage** corresponds to the integration and functional consolidation of the platform. During this phase, the technological components developed in the project will be integrated into a functional web-based platform designed for procurement evaluation teams. The system will incorporate document processing capabilities, structured evidence extraction, and traceability mechanisms that allow evaluators to verify the evidentiary basis of system outputs. This stage will ensure that the platform can operate within realistic institutional workflows and support the analytical needs of procurement evaluation committees.

The **third stage** involves experimental validation in real operational conditions. The system will be tested through pilot deployments in selected procurement processes conducted by SERVIU RM. The validation will operate under a shadow-mode framework, where the system processes procurement documentation and generates analytical outputs in parallel with the official evaluation conducted by human committees. This approach allows the project team to measure key performance indicators such as evaluation concordance, processing time reduction, evidence coverage, and usability for institutional users, while ensuring that all formal administrative decisions remain under human authority.

Following successful validation, **the fourth stage** focuses on knowledge transfer and institutional adoption. CENIA will implement a structured capability transfer program aimed at supporting the adoption of the system within SERVIU RM. This program will include technical training sessions for procurement officials, operational guidelines for system use, and documentation of system architecture and traceability mechanisms. These activities will enable institutional users to understand, operate, and critically evaluate the technology within their existing procurement workflows. The transfer process will also establish the institutional conditions required for sustained system use beyond the duration of the research project.

Finally, the strategic development plan contemplates a pathway for progressive institutional scaling following project completion. Once validated within SERVIU RM, the technology may be replicated across other regional SERVIU offices operating under the MINVU, which share similar procurement procedures and regulatory frameworks. This sectorial expansion strategy allows the system to be deployed within a homogeneous institutional environment, facilitating the generation of comparable operational evidence while minimizing implementation risks. In the longer term, the system may expand toward additional public institutions conducting document-intensive procurement evaluations, as well as toward potential interoperability mechanisms with the national procurement platform Mercado Público administered by ChileCompra.

**Technology Transfer Strategy and Institutional Adoption Plan**

The technology transfer strategy of the project is structured around a staged institutional adoption model, beginning with controlled validation within SERVIU RM and progressively enabling broader adoption across public institutions conducting procurement evaluation processes. SERVIU RM will act as the primary early adopter and validation partner, providing the institutional environment required to test the system under real operational conditions. During the project execution period, CENIA will implement a structured knowledge and capability transfer program aimed at ensuring that procurement teams within SERVIU RM can effectively understand, operate, and evaluate the system. This program will include technical workshops, hands-on training sessions for procurement officials, documentation of system architecture and evaluation traceability protocols, and the development of operational guidelines for integrating AI-assisted admissibility verification and evaluation support tools into existing procurement workflows. In parallel, the project contemplates the formalization of institutional collaboration agreements between CENIA and SERVIU RM. These agreements will define roles and responsibilities related to system use, data governance standards, cybersecurity safeguards, and operational continuity following project completion. Such agreements will establish the institutional framework required to transition from pilot validation toward sustained operational use of the technology within procurement evaluation processes. While the software developed in the project will remain the intellectual property of CENIA, the long-term operational model of the platform is expected to follow a GovTech Software-as-a-Service framework. This approach enables scalable deployment across public institutions through institutional service agreements, while maintaining centralized technical maintenance, system updates, and regulatory adaptation. 

**Long-Term Sustainability, Post-Project Roadmap, and Institutional Scaling**

Upon completion of the project and successful validation under real operational conditions at SERVIU RM, the system is expected to reach Technology Readiness Level 7 (TRL 7), corresponding to a technology demonstrated and validated in a relevant institutional environment. At this stage, the results of the project will provide the technical and operational evidence required to support broader institutional adoption. The long-term sustainability of the proposed system will be supported by CENIA’s institutional capacity to maintain, evolve, and transfer advanced AI-based systems beyond the duration of the research project. Following successful validation, CENIA will establish a dedicated product and maintenance team responsible for system operation, regulatory monitoring, feature improvement, and institutional onboarding processes. This team will operate within CENIA’s technology transfer and innovation activities, leveraging the center’s experience in managing technology transfer agreements and collaborative projects with public institutions and industry partners.

A key element of long-term operational sustainability is the capacity of the system to remain aligned with evolving regulatory frameworks governing public procurement. To support this requirement, the system architecture incorporates mechanisms that allow the updating and reconfiguration of admissibility and evaluation rules when regulatory modifications occur, including changes to the Chilean public procurement legislation (Law No. 19,886) and related regulatory directives. From a technical perspective, the platform will be deployed through a hybrid infrastructure model combining CENIA’s internal computational resources with secure external cloud services, ensuring scalability, cybersecurity compliance, and operational reliability compatible with institutional requirements. This infrastructure enables the system to support document-intensive analytical workflows while maintaining secure data processing environments aligned with public-sector standards. The long-term operational model of the platform is expected to follow a Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) institutional deployment scheme, allowing public institutions to access the system through secure web-based infrastructure while benefiting from centralized system maintenance, regulatory updates, and technical support. Under this framework, the continued operation of the platform will rely on institutional service agreements with adopting organizations, enabling sustainable maintenance and technological evolution beyond the research phase.

In parallel, CENIA and SERVIU RM are preparing to formalize a broader institutional collaboration agreement that will enable the continuation of pilot deployments and the testing of complementary functionalities related to additional stages of the public procurement workflow. This post-project collaboration framework will allow the generation of additional operational, technical, and institutional evidence regarding the use of AI-assisted analytical tools in procurement evaluation processes, supporting decision-making regarding broader institutional adoption. Engagement with the Dirección de Compras y Contratación Pública (ChileCompra) will also be explored as part of the long-term diffusion strategy. Given ChileCompra’s role in administering the national procurement platform Mercado Público, collaboration with this institution may enable the exploration of interoperability mechanisms, regulatory alignment, and potential national-level diffusion pathways for analytical tools that support procurement evaluation processes. Any such integration would be subject to institutional governance frameworks and regulatory feasibility assessments.

From an international perspective, CENIA maintains established collaborations with multilateral organizations including the Inter-American Development Bank (IDB/BID), the World Bank, and the OECD, which actively promote digital transformation and modernization of public procurement systems. In particular, initiatives such as the Inter-American Network of Government Procurement (RICG), supported by the Organization of American States and the IDB, provide potential channels for disseminating technological approaches that improve transparency and efficiency in procurement processes. Through its participation in regional initiatives such as the Latin American Artificial Intelligence Index (ILIA) and the development of LatamGPT, CENIA is positioned as a recognized actor in Latin America’s digital transformation ecosystem.

**Chapter 2: Objectives, Results and Work Plan** 

**2.1 General and specific objectives**

**2.1.1 General Objective:**

Develop an Agentic System capable of making Chilean public procurement processes more efficient, without compromising transparency and trust and complying with current regulations, through automatically detecting inadmissible proposals and providing informed prior scores during the evaluation phase. Ultimately, we will make the state contracting process more efficient, thereby improving its policy implementation capacity.

**2.1.2 Specific Objectives:**

1. Characterize and model the institutional process for evaluating public tenders at SERVIU Metropolitano, identifying workflows, eligibility criteria, and evaluation criteria used in practice.

2. Design and develop a technological architecture based on multi-agent systems capable of supporting key tasks in the evaluation workflow, including proposal filtering and evaluation support.

3. Integrate the technological components developed into a functional platform that supports the work of the evaluation committees through document analysis tools, requirements verification, and evidence organization.

4. Validate the system experimentally in collaboration with SERVIU Metropolitano through pilot tests in real bidding processes under a shadow-mode scheme.

5. Transfer the knowledge generated to the SERVIU Metropolitano team through training and technical support activities that facilitate the adoption of the developed system and strengthen institutional capacities for the use of AI in the evaluation of public tenders.

**2.2** **Technological Results Compromised and others**

**2.2.1 Committed or expected Technological Result:** 

| Technological Result |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| Name. | Puelche: Agentic AI Decision-Support Platform for Public Procurement Evaluation  |
| Date of achievement. | Month 13 |
| Indicate what it is or what it is made of, detail all the components. | The technological result is an operational software platform implementing a multi-agent AI system that supports bid evaluation processes in SERVIU RM. The system includes: **1\. Data Extraction Module:** OCR pipeline, document preprocessing, format normalization, structured text conversion (Markdown/JSON). **2\. Proposal Filtering Module:** admissibility verification engine, rule-based and LLM-assisted compliance checking of bids, and justification generation. **3\. Evaluation Support Module:** extracts evaluation criteria, organizing bidder evidence, and structuring it according to the tender’s scoring rules. Provides traceable support for each criterion while leaving all final judgments and decisions to human evaluators. **4\. Compliance Skills and Tools Library:** shared capability layer that provides modular, reusable skills and computational tools enabling agents in the system to perform specialized procurement analysis tasks through standardized, interoperable, and auditable procedures. **5\. User Interface & Traceability Layer:** web platform, document-evidence linking, audit trail logging, human validation interface, export tools. **6\. Optimization Loop:** iterative improvement framework for the agentic modules. The process progressively improves the reliability, coverage, and alignment with real procurement evaluation practices. |
| Please indicate whether previous results are protected, what type of intellectual property protection you have, or how you plan to protect the results of the project. | The project builds upon prior research and prototype developments that are owned by CENIA. These prior results consist primarily of software components and experimental prototypes that are not publicly distributed and are maintained as proprietary research assets. The technology is being developed nationally. The results of the proposed project will be protected primarily through copyright protection of the software, confidentiality of the source code, and trade secrets associated with the system architecture, data processing pipelines, and agent orchestration methods. Because the system will be deployed under a SaaS model, the core technology will not be distributed to end users; instead, users will interact with the platform through controlled interfaces. This deployment model naturally protects the intellectual property by preventing direct access to the underlying algorithms, source code, and infrastructure. Additionally, the project partners will regulate access and use of the technology through software licensing agreements, service contracts, and confidentiality agreements with institutional partners and pilot users. If specific technical components developed during the project demonstrate significant novelty and patentability, the consortium will evaluate the possibility of filing patent applications to protect key technological innovations. |
| Indicate and justify the starting TRL and the TRL or level of development that will be reached. | At the time of project submission, the **Puelche system is at Technology Readiness Level (TRL) 4**, corresponding to technology validated in a laboratory environment. A functional prototype of the **Evaluation Support Module** has already been implemented and tested on real procurement documents from multiple tender contexts. These tests demonstrate the technical feasibility of applying an **AI-assisted agent-based architecture** to support complex documentary evaluation tasks in this process, validating the core technical components of the system under controlled conditions. The proposed project will extend this prototype by implementing the remaining architectural modules, expanding the analytical coverage of the **Evaluation Support Module**, and integrating the system into a coherent platform capable of supporting the broader procurement evaluation workflow.
