Through these developments and its validation in collaboration with **SERVIU RM**, the project is expected to reach **TRL 7**, corresponding to a **system prototype demonstrated in an operational environment**, enabling real-world institutional validation and preparing the technology for precommercial deployment. |
| What type of validations will be carried out, what type of tests, how many repetitions, under what treatment conditions, what will the controls be. | The validation of the proposed system will be conducted through a structured multi-stage evaluation process in collaboration with SERVIU RM, combining retrospective benchmarking with controlled operational pilot tests. **1\. Retrospective Validation with Historical Procurement Data**During the initial validation phase, the system will be tested using historical public tender datasets provided by SERVIU RM. These datasets will enable controlled benchmarking against previously completed evaluation processes. The validation will consist of repeated evaluation runs across multiple historical tenders. For each tender, the system will process the same documentation originally reviewed by human evaluation committees. Each tender will be processed multiple times under controlled parameter settings to evaluate robustness and sensitivity of the system’s decision-support outputs. **2\. Shadow-Mode Operational Validation**Following retrospective validation, the system will be deployed in a **shadow-mode validation framework** during 10 selected ongoing public procurement processes conducted by SERVIU RM. In this configuration, the system operates in parallel with human evaluation committees without influencing official decisions. For each participating tender process, the system will independently perform admissibility analysis and evaluation support tasks using the same documentation provided to human evaluators. The results generated by the system will then be compared with the outcomes produced by the official evaluation committees. Human evaluation outcomes will serve as the **primary control baseline**, allowing direct comparison between traditional evaluation workflows and AI-assisted analysis. This phase will be repeated across multiple tender processes to capture variability in procurement complexity, documentation formats, and evaluation criteria. **3\. Economic Impact Assessment**An applied comparative validation will be conducted to assess the operational effect of Puelche under real procurement conditions. The validation will combine a historical baseline treatment and a live pilot treatment: the control condition will be the conventional manual evaluation process, estimated from a multi-year sample of past tenders, while the treatment condition will be the AI-assisted evaluation process using Puelche during the pilot. The tests will consist of repeated transaction-cost estimations at the tender level, comparing manual versus AI-assisted evaluations across multiple procurement cases; each tender evaluated constitutes one repetition. The validation will therefore examine differences in person-hours and, administrative cost per tender under both conditions. **4\. User and Institutional Validation**Additionally we will assess **user experience and institutional acceptance** through structured evaluations, a combination of observation, focus groups, and structured surveys, involving the users of the system, focusing on perceived usefulness, reliability, transparency, trust in system outputs, and alignment with existing evaluation practices. In parallel, **leadership and organizational satisfaction** will be evaluated through targeted assessments with supervisory and institutional stakeholders, examining perceived value in terms of decision quality, procedural consistency, transparency, and alignment with organizational objectives and accountability requirements. |
| Explain and justify the indicators (quantitative and qualitative) that are expected to be achieved and why this is better (competitive advantage) than the competitor's value. | **Puelche’s Quantitative Indicators Efficacy** **Inadmissibility Accuracy:** Degree to which the system correctly identifies admissible and inadmissible bids, measured as the proportion of system classifications that match expert human evaluations (or validated ground truth) across procurement cases, compared against historical baseline performance under manual evaluation processes. **Evaluation Inter-Rater Reliability:** Agreement between Puelche-generated assessments and evaluations conducted independently by domain specialists in the team qualifications section of the technical requirements evaluation, measured using Cohen’s Kappa, Pearson correlation, and F1 score. **Evaluation Criteria Coverage:** Extent to which the system supports the full set of defined technical evaluation criteria, measured as the number of criteria types operationalized by Puelche that also meet the inter-rater reliability threshold. **Economic** **Evaluation Time Reduction (Filtering and Evaluation):** Difference between the average time required for proposal filtering and technical evaluation using Puelche and the average time required by professional teams under standard manual procedures. Each process independently and in conjunction. **Cost of Evaluation Reduction:** Difference between the historical average cost per proposal evaluation and the average cost per evaluation using Puelche, reported as both monetary savings and percentage change. **Puelche’s Qualitative Indicators User Experience and Institutional Acceptance:** In-depth evaluation of user satisfaction among Evaluation Committee members and Acquisition personnel, examining perceived usefulness, reliability, transparency, trust in system outputs, and alignment with existing evaluation practices. **Leadership and Organizational Satisfaction:** Assessment of satisfaction and perceived value among supervisory and institutional leadership, focusing on decision quality, procedural consistency, transparency, and alignment with organizational objectives and accountability requirements. While comparable systems exist, they are limited to simpler functionalities (e.g., RAG-based assistance or rule-based checks) and do not address the full complexity of agent-based bid evaluation. These solutions will therefore be used as baseline references, with performance assessed relative to both their capabilities and the added complexity of the proposed system. |
| Attribute\*. Indicate values or ranges of values to be achieved (numbers, percentages, decimals, other measurable). | **1\. Inadmissibility Accuracy**: 75–95%. **2\. Evaluation Inter-Rater Reliability**: 80% F1 score.. **3\. Evaluation Time Reduction (Filtering and Evaluation)**: 40% reduction. **4\. Cost per Evaluation Reduction**: 40% reduction. **5\. Coverage of evaluation criteria:** 20 Puelche’s key advantage is its flexibility to incorporate diverse evaluation criteria, unlike simpler systems with fixed or limited schemas. |

| :---- | :---- |
| Name of the milestone. | Development and Technical Validation of the Puelche AI-assisted Platform  |
| Date of achievement. | Month 10  |
| Aim. | To complete the development, integration, and internal validation of the Puelche platform, including the Data Extraction, Proposal Filtering, Evaluation Support, and User Interface modules, ensuring the system can process procurement documentation and assist evaluation committees according to Chilean procurement regulations. |
| Critical variables to be studied. | **1\. Inadmissibility Accuracy:** Degree to which the system correctly identifies admissible and inadmissible bids, measured as the proportion of system classifications that match expert human evaluations (or validated ground truth) across procurement cases, compared against historical baseline performance under manual evaluation processes. **2\. Evaluation Inter-Rater Reliability:** Agreement between Puelche-generated assessments and evaluations conducted independently by domain specialists in the team qualifications section of the technical requirements evaluation, measured using Cohen’s Kappa, Pearson correlation, and F1 score. **3\. Processing time per proposal**: The time it takes the system to analyze one proposal, calculated as the average time required for the system to process a proposal compared. **4\. Cost per proposal processed**, evaluating the operational cost associated with analyzing a proposal using the platform, calculated as the average computational and tokens cost required for the system to process a single proposal, including model inference, document processing, and supporting infrastructure resources. |
| Explanation and justification of the value ranges that are planned to be achieved. | **1\. Inadmissibility Accuracy**Achieve **an accuracy range of 75–95%** in the correct classification of admissible and inadmissible bids, as measured against expert human evaluations ground truth. This metric reflects the system’s ability to reliably operationalize admissibility rules and reduce classification errors. **2\. Evaluation Inter-Rater Reliability** Achieve an **F1-score range of 70–90%** in the agreement between Puelche’s evaluation outputs and expert human assessments across supported technical evaluation criteria. This estimate is informed by results reported in related, though simpler, AI-assisted document evaluation tasks, as well as by the current performance observed in the prototype. **3\. Processing Time per Proposal**The **average processing time can take between 1 and 8 hours per proposal**. This range reflects expected variability depending on document length and complexity while still representing a substantial efficiency improvement over manual review. **4\. Cost per Proposal Processed:**The operational target is **US$2–10 per proposal processed**. This range reflects anticipated token consumption and computational costs in large-scale institutional use. |
| Brief description of the experiments, treatments and/or tests to be performed. | The evaluation will consist of controlled experiments using historical public tender processes not used during system development to ensure an unbiased assessment. The system will be tested under two treatment conditions: (i) fully automated execution on historical cases, and (ii) AI-assisted evaluation scenarios reflecting real operational use. For each condition, multiple procurement cases will be processed to assess admissibility classification accuracy and inter-rater reliability against prior human-reviewed outcomes. In parallel, system performance tests will be conducted to measure average processing time per proposal and operational cost per proposal, enabling a comparative analysis of efficiency and effectiveness across treatments. |
| Main results expected to be achieved. | The milestone is expected to demonstrate that the Puelche platform achieves operational performance levels compatible with real institutional procurement workflows. Specifically, the system should meet or exceed the defined minimum performance thresholds for rule identification accuracy, traceable analytical outputs, and computational efficiency. Achieving these targets will provide empirical evidence that the technology is sufficiently mature to proceed to shadow-mode institutional testing, where the system will operate alongside human evaluation committees without affecting official decisions. This stage will enable validation under real operational conditions and initiate the technological transfer process toward institutional adoption. |
| Attribute. Indicate values or ranges of values to be achieved for this milestone (numbers, percentages, decimals, other measurable). | **1\. Inadmissibility Accuracy:** More than 85% **2\. Evaluation Consistency Rate:** 80% F1 score.  **3\. Processing Time per Proposal:** Less than 2 hours. **4\. Cost per Proposal Processed:**Below US$5 per proposal. **5\. Coverage of evaluation criteria:** 15 |

| Date of achievement. | Month 22 |
| Aim. | To deploy the Puelche platform within SERVIU Metropolitano and validate its performance in real procurement evaluation processes, ensuring institutional integration, operational usability, and measurable efficiency improvements. |
| Critical variables to be studied. | **1\. Evaluation Time Reduction (Filtering and Evaluation):** Difference between the average time required for proposal filtering and technical evaluation using Puelche and the average time required by professional teams under standard manual procedures. Each process independently and in conjunction. **2\. Cost per Evaluation Reduction:** Difference between the historical average cost per proposal evaluation and the average cost per evaluation using Puelche, reported as both monetary savings and percentage change. **3\. User Experience and Institutional Acceptance:** In-depth evaluation of user satisfaction among Evaluation Committee members and Acquisition personnel, examining perceived usefulness, reliability, transparency, trust in system outputs, and alignment with existing evaluation practices. **4\. Leadership and Organizational Satisfaction:** Assessment of satisfaction and perceived value among supervisory and institutional leadership, focusing on decision quality, procedural consistency, transparency, and alignment with organizational objectives and accountability requirements. **5\. Evaluation Time Reduction (Filtering and Evaluation):** A target reduction in the range of **25%–50%** is expected. This range reflects realistic efficiency gains from automating document screening, admissibility checks, and structured evidence extraction, while preserving necessary human validation stages. Variability depends on tender complexity and document volume. **2\. Cost per Evaluation Reduction:** A reduction of **25%–50%** is anticipated when excluding platform development and fixed infrastructure costs. Savings derive from decreased person-hours and reduced administrative burden. The range accounts for differences in tender size, evaluator seniority, and institutional overhead structures. **3\. User Experience and Institutional Acceptance:** Target average satisfaction scores in the range of **3.5–4.5 (on a 5-point Likert scale)**. This reflects the expectation that the system will be perceived as useful and trustworthy, while acknowledging an initial adaptation phase and the need to build confidence in AI-assisted evaluation workflows. **4\. Leadership and Organizational Satisfaction:** Expected satisfaction levels between **4.0–4.5 (on a 5-point Likert scale)**. Leadership-level evaluation typically emphasizes transparency, auditability, and consistency; therefore, slightly higher acceptance is expected due to the system’s structured outputs and traceability mechanisms. **5\. System Adoption:** A progressive adoption rate is expected, reaching over **80% of evaluation processes** using the platform during the validation phase. Additionally, a **conversion rate (≥50%) from document uploads to exported outputs** is targeted, indicating that system-generated analyses are not only initiated but also deemed sufficiently useful for downstream use. This range reflects gradual institutional integration and learning curves of technology adoption. |

Each tender constitutes one repetition, and the tests will estimate and compare person-hours and administrative cost per evaluation across both conditions. **2\. User and institutional validation,** carried out through structured observation, focus groups, and user surveys and institutional stakeholders, assessing perceived usefulness, reliability, transparency, trust in outputs, decision quality, and alignment with existing procedures. **3\. System adoption tests,** validated using usage logs, tracking document uploads and generated outputs, and the proportion of procurement processes in which the system is actively used. |

This approach will foster interdisciplinary researchers who possess both the computational skills to implement advanced AI and the analytical framework to understand its impact on public administration, enabling them to design rigorous, data-driven solutions for state institutions. By streamlining administrative workflows and providing targeted training programs, public officials will be equipped to apply AI efficiently, critically, and ethically. **2.3 Experimental Design**

The project proposes one bounded technological object: a human-supervised, AI-assisted system for the first documentary review of bidder submissions in selected public procurement evaluation tasks. The purpose of the experimental design is therefore to determine whether the system can (i) organize tender and bidder documents into review-ready evidence, (ii) generate structured task-level packets that are useful to evaluators, and (iii) improve the speed, consistency, and reduce the transaction cost of the first review stage while preserving traceability and human control. The methodology combines workflow diagnosis with SERVIU RM, controlled retrospective validation on authorized case materials, and shadow-mode demonstration in a real institutional environment.

The experimental design is organized in four phases. Phase 1 assesses the current public procurement process, the workflow boundary, and the curated historical training dataset. Phase 2 validates the prototype under controlled conditions on prior cases and decides whether it is ready to enter shadow mode. Phase 3 tests operational usefulness and workflow fit in parallel with human review Phase 4 conducts ex post impact evaluations to test the product's main hypothesis. This sequence is designed to ensure that the project proceeds to a relevant operational demonstration only after the prototype has demonstrated adequate performance.

Because the methodology includes interviews, focus groups, and surveys with public officials, the research team will submit all human-subject instruments to the relevant Institutional Scientific Ethics Committee before fieldwork, and apply informed consent, confidentiality, and restricted access procedures. The project will also clearly distinguish between public procurement records, internal institutional materials, bidder documents that may contain sensitive information, and user-generated research data, such as interviews or surveys. Access to the historical dataset and to shadow-mode cases will proceed only under the data-governance and authorization instruments agreed with SERVIU and current Governmental Information Security regulations.

Across the three phases, the project will prioritize a small set of performance measures tied directly to the product claim. The main metrics are inter-rater reliability using prior human-reviewed outcomes on comparable tasks, citation quality, repeatability under fixed conditions, time to first structured review packet, and reviewer usefulness in shadow mode. Furthermore, to validate the product's goals on improving administrative efficiency, we will collect data on SERVIU’s workload and transaction costs. Additional technical diagnostics, such as OCR quality, tool-calling reliability, processing time, compute cost, and system error logs, will be used to improve the system.

The following figure summarizes the complete four-stage validation sequence and methodology.

![][image1]

**Phase 1: Ex-ante Diagnosis (0% Development) – Diagnosis of public procurement processes and collection of qualitative and quantitative information.**

Phase 1 establishes the project's institutional and analytical baseline. Its purpose is to diagnose current administrative efficiency, understand how document review is currently performed in practice, and finalize baseline measures to support later validation. This phase will be led by the principal investigator, with support from the research team and the designated SERVIU counterparts, who will participate in workflow mapping, process clarification, and dataset curation.

The quantitative component of Phase 1 will characterize the timing and workload patterns of public tender processes at the national level and, more specifically, in the SERVIU RM context. Using Mercado Público open API data and institutionally relevant process information, the project will identify basic patterns in publication-to-award timing, delay concentration, tender types, and procurement-process burden. To deepen this analysis, we will conduct a person-hour survey to measure the time officials dedicate to each tender across the agency. By cross-referencing these hours with salary data and awarded amounts, we will estimate the transaction costs of SERVIU’s procurement processes, following the methodologies of Balaeva et al. (2022) and PwC (2011). This baseline will draw on a multi-year historical view of public tenders and will be complemented by institutional timing and workload information. The purpose of this quantitative work is to provide a grounded baseline for understanding where document review costs are concentrated and to have baseline data against which to compare the efficiency and cost of our system once it has been developed.

The qualitative component will focus on how evaluation work is actually carried out inside SERVIU RM. The project will conduct interviews, workshops, and focus groups with officials involved in tender evaluation to identify the review tasks that consume the most time, the parts of the workflow where documentary burden is highest, the evidence practices that evaluators rely on, and the conditions under which a reviewer would consider the system useful and trustworthy**.** Additionally, we will present the pilot results and the proposed technical design to gather early feedback from these key stakeholders. These activities will clarify which review tasks can be tested fairly and what form reviewer-facing outputs should take to align with existing practice.

Phase 1 will be considered complete only when five elements are finalized: the workflow map, the historical training dataset, the baseline manual-review timing and cost measure, and the applicable data-access and governance instrument. These outputs define the conditions for controlled retrospective validation in Phase 2\.

**Phase 2: In itinere Validation (0% \- 60% Development) – Performance monitoring through historical feeding and the construction of relevant metrics.**

Phase 2 is the controlled validation phase. Its purpose is to refine and test the integrated prototype on historical case materials before the system is exposed to shadow-mode use. The principal investigator and research team will lead this phase, delivering continuous feedback to the technical team throughout Puelche’s development. Meanwhile, SERVIU will supply the institutional context and feedback needed to ensure the comparison setup and outputs align with the intended workflow.

The phase begins with foundational technical validation of the system’s upstream components. These checks will confirm that the platform can ingest bid attachments, recover text and layout from tender documents, extract rules and evaluation criteria, identify relevant evidentiary material, and reliably call the required computational functions. This includes validation of document extraction quality, layout-aware processing, and tool-calling reliability under controlled test conditions. In practice, this stage will include unit testing for individual functionalities of the system, and integration, load, and stress tests for the whole system. The goal of these checks is to ensure that the system can build a review-ready case file before any substantive claim is made about its support for eligibility checks or evaluation tasks.

Once these foundational components reach acceptable thresholds, the project will transition to retrospective, task-level validation. In this stage, the system will process historical procurement cases and produce task-level review packets under frozen settings whenever formal comparison is required.

Between retrospective validation rounds, the project will improve the system through versioned, offline refinement. This means that we may revise prompts, review procedures, document preparation logic, and assignment rules between runs. Still, the system will not learn or adapt on the fly during a formal evaluation. The purpose of this refinement loop is to identify where the prototype fails, correct those weaknesses in a controlled way, and test whether the next version is more reliable on later material.

To evaluate reliability, the primary indicator in Phase 2 will be the inter-rater reliability between the system's outputs and prior human-reviewed outcomes. This approach has become a gold standard for validating LLMs against expert human baselines across various complex domains, including qualitative research (Dunivin, 2025), medical education (Mishra et al., 2025), oncology (Kristjánsson et al., 2026), systematic reviews (Woelfle et al., 2024), and LLM-as-a-judge coding tasks (Crupi et al., 2025). Specifically, we will measure this agreement using: Cohen’s Kappa for dichotomous variables (e.g., whether a bid includes an gender plan) (Cohen, 1960); Quadratic Weighted Cohen’s Kappa for ordinal variables, which heavily penalizes larger discrepancies (Cohen, 1968), and, F1 Score to measure matches, treating the historical human evaluation as the ground truth.

Beyond these core agreement metrics, Phase 2 will track citation quality, repeatability under fixed conditions, the share of tasks left open for human review, and time to first structured output. We will continuously analyze misaligned Puelche runs to identify and counteract unintended patterns, such as subjective biases and discrimination. Secondary diagnostics, such as token usage, OCR quality, tool-calling reliability, processing time, compute cost, and structured error logs, will be tracked to guide refinement, identify technical bottlenecks, and support version-to-version comparisons.

Phase 2 will end only when the integrated system meets the minimum thresholds required for shadow mode. If this is not the case, the project will remain in retrospective validation until the identified problems are resolved.

**Phase 3: Integration into SERVIU’s live evaluation process (60% \- 95% Development) – Shadow testing and user satisfaction evaluation.**

Phase 3 is the operational demonstration phase. Its purpose is to test the prototype in an institutional environment relevant to its intended use, while preserving the formal human decision-making path. The system will therefore operate in shadow-mode alongside SERVIU evaluators rather than replacing the official process. This phase is designed to answer a different question from Phase 2: not only whether the system can align with prior outcomes under controlled conditions, but also whether its outputs are useful, understandable, and operationally valuable to the people who would actually use them.

During this phase, the project will work with selected SERVIU cases and participating reviewers under an agreed shadow-mode protocol. The system will prepare task-level packets and case summaries in parallel with the normal evaluation process, without affecting the official administrative result. Users will receive training on interpreting the outputs, reviewing the cited evidence, and providing structured feedback on the system’s usefulness, limitations, and fit with existing practice. The other objective of this phase is to evaluate the user-facing layer of the platform, including clarity of the packets, traceability of the cited evidence, and the ease with which reviewers can identify what still requires human judgment. Case selection in this phase will aim to capture variation in procurement complexity, documentation burden, and task mix, so that the shadow-mode assessment is not limited to a single tender type.

User evaluation will be assessed through structured evaluations, a combination of observation, focus groups, and structured surveys, involving the users of the system, focusing on perceived usefulness, reliability, transparency, trust in system outputs, and alignment with existing evaluation practices. In parallel, leadership and organizational satisfaction will be evaluated through targeted assessments with supervisory and institutional stakeholders, examining perceived value in terms of decision quality, procedural consistency, transparency, and alignment with organizational objectives and accountability requirements.

Ultimately, this phase will culminate in four key deliverables: a shadow-mode evaluation memo, a user and institutional feedback summary, an operational fit assessment, and a strategic set of readiness recommendations. These outputs will serve as the final validation gate, determining if the system is fully mature and trustworthy enough to enter live production in the final phase.

**Phase 4: Assisted Live-Pilot and Ex-post Evaluation (95% \- 100% Development) – Production deployment and final assessment of product achievements.**

Phase 4 serves as the definitive deployment and assessment of the project's impact. Having successfully passed the qualitative and shadow-mode readiness checks in Phase 3, the system will transition into an assisted live pilot. During this stage, participating committees will officially use Puelche's generated packets as the starting point for their formal decisions, effectively putting the system into production.

This phase is designed to definitively answer three core operational questions:

1. Has the system maintained high accuracy in a production environment?  
2. Has it accelerated the official awarding timeline?  
3. Has it successfully reduced administrative procurement costs?

To answer these questions, accuracy will be validated via inter-rater reliability while timeline acceleration will be measured by contrasting historical API data against Puelche’s live performance metrics. To address the third question regarding costs, the operational impact assessment will execute a rigorous four-step economic evaluation. First, we will retrieve the baseline transaction costs calculated in Phase 1 (evaluator salaries multiplied by manual person-hours per tender). Second, we will replicate this person-hour survey to calculate the new transaction costs for official evaluations conducted by Puelche users during the live pilot. Third, we will aggregate Puelche’s operational overhead (e.g., cloud infrastructure and LLM token costs) to the new transaction costs. Finally, the net difference between the baseline manual cost and the combined AI-assisted operational cost yields the precise administrative savings generated by the system.

Beyond administrative efficiency, Phase 4 includes a summative algorithmic audit. We will conduct a deep-dive analysis of a subset of mismatched cases to formally validate the continuous bias-mitigation and non-discrimination monitoring actively performed during Phases 2 and 3\. Finally, this phase will establish a continuous monitoring strategy, deploying long-term usage metrics, automated reporting, and in-app feedback mechanisms to ensure the application's ongoing sustainability.

**2.4.2 Budget**

The requested budget is primarily allocated to three critical components directly aligned with the execution of the work plan. First, the majority of the funds will support a multidisciplinary technical team responsible for the design, development, and validation of the Puelche platform. Second, resources are assigned to cloud computing infrastructure and LLM token usage, which are essential for document processing, agentic workflows, and system evaluation under realistic operational conditions. Finally, a portion of the budget is dedicated to travel expenses to disseminate research outcomes.

 **2.5.2 Gender equity, human capital formation and R\&D\&I capabilities.**

The project will address gender equity through the composition of its extended team and through the incorporation of young researchers during execution. Although the core research team at the beneficiary institution is composed primarily of male researchers, the project is being developed in close collaboration with SERVIU RM, whose project team includes a majority of female professionals actively participating in the co-design, validation, and institutional implementation of the proposed technology. In parallel, CENIA’s collaboration agreements with more than twenty universities create favorable conditions for incorporating undergraduate and graduate students into applied R\&D activities. Through these channels, the project will seek to expand opportunities for female participation in AI-related areas. Gender diversity will also be considered in the selection of institutional participants involved in user-facing activities, so that the evaluation of the technology incorporates diverse perspectives from within the public administration ecosystem.

The project will also contribute to human capital formation by integrating young researchers and students into the development and validation of the system. In line with the outputs described in Section 2.2.4, it will support at least one undergraduate thesis and one master’s thesis linked to the project’s technological and institutional challenges. These activities may address topics such as multi-agent AI systems, document intelligence pipelines, NLP applied to regulatory documents, and administrative efficiency in public procurement. Through participation in the project, students will gain practical experience in the design, implementation, and validation of advanced AI systems in real government environments.

Finally, the project will strengthen R\&D\&I capabilities in both the beneficiary institution and the associated entity. Within CENIA, it will reinforce capacities in AI-assisted public sector innovation and contribute reusable technological assets, including an open dataset of Chilean public procurement processes to be hosted on the OpenCENIA platform. Within SERVIU RM, the project will strengthen institutional capacity to engage with transparent and traceable AI-based decision-support tools through co-design, validation, and targeted training activities, supporting the responsible adoption of these technologies in public administration.

**Use of Large Language Models (LLMs) in Proposal Preparation**

This proposal was prepared with responsible use of LLMs as writing aids, supporting clarity, grammar, summarization, translation, and content organization. All substantive ideas, technical design, and scientific judgments were developed and validated by the authors. LLMs served strictly as editorial support, without influencing the scientific content, preserving full human responsibility for all intellectual contributions.

**ANNEX: Technology Readiness Level**

 

This is a type of measurement used to assess the maturity level of a particular technology. Each technology project can be analyzed and categorized according to the parameters of each technology level and then assigned a rating based on the technological progress of the research result. For the purposes of this call, we will identify this level of advancement or progress with which the projects apply and with which they hope to conclude once the ANID grant has ended. As a summary, 9 levels are considered that extend from the basic principles of the new technology to its successful testing in a real environment: 

 

**TRL 1 \- Basic principles studied:** This corresponds to the lowest level in terms of technological maturity. Basic scientific research begins. The transition to applied research begins. At this stage of development there is still no degree of commercial application. 

**TRL 2 \- Technological concept formulated:** In this phase, possible applications of the technologies and analytical tools for the simulation or analysis of the application can begin to be formulated. However, there are still no tests or analyses available to validate said application. 

**TRL 3 \- Experimental proof of concept:** This phase involves the performance of research and development (R\&D) activities, including analytical testing and laboratory scale testing aimed at demonstrating the technical feasibility of the technological concepts. This phase involves the validation of the components of a specific technology, although this does not lead to the integration of all the components into a complete system. 

**TRL 4 \- Laboratory validated technology:** In this phase, the components that make up a certain technology have been identified and the aim is to establish whether these individual components have the capacity to act in an integrated manner, working together in a system. 

**TRL 5 \- Technology validated in a relevant environment:** The basic elements of a given technology are integrated so that the final configuration is similar to its final application. However, the operation of the system and technologies still occurs at laboratory level. 

**TRL 6 \- Technology demonstrated in a relevant environment:** At this stage, it is possible to have pilot prototypes capable of developing all the necessary functions within a given system, having passed feasibility tests in real operating conditions. Components and processes may have been scaled up to demonstrate their industrial potential in real systems. 

**TRL 7 \- Demonstration of complete system or prototype demonstrated in operational environment:** The system is or is close to operating on a pre-commercial scale. It is possible to carry out the identification phase of aspects related to manufacturing, life cycle assessment, and economic evaluation of the technologies, with most of the functions available and tested. 

**TRL 8 \- Complete system certified through testing and demonstration:** In this phase, the technologies have been tested in their final form and under operational conditions, having reached in many cases, the end of system development. 

**TRL 9 \- Real system tested in a real operational environment:** Technology/system in its final phase, tested and available for commercialization and/or production. 

[^1]:  See the accompanying research report (located in Attached Materials \> TRL \> TR4 Supporting Research Report.docx in the accompanying Google Drive, alongside a replication repository) for the evidence underpinning the claims in this section: Sections 2 and 4 describe the system architecture and end-to-end workflow, Section 3 documents the benchmark dataset and case types, Section 5 describes the validation studies, and Section 6 reports the resulting performance on the admitted task set.

[^2]:  Note: Titles of non-English patents (e.g., Chinese registrations) reflect official or machine translations provided by the respective search engines.
